title: Grammar · English A1
scope: By the end of A1 you can use the verb to be, subject pronouns and possessive adjectives, the present simple and present continuous, articles and plurals, there is/are, have got, can for ability, and basic question words and prepositions. Everything is in the present: the past tense is not covered yet.

blocks.text: The most basic verb. It has three present forms and is used for identity, description and location.
Negative: I am not / he isn't / they aren't. Question: Are you ready? Is he here?
This is my book. Her name is Anna.
For habits, routines and facts. The third person singular (he/she/it) adds -s.
Negatives and questions use do/does: I don't work. Does she work? — verbs add -es after -o, -ch, -sh, -ss (goes, watches).
For actions happening now or around now. Formed with am/is/are + verb-ing.
I am reading. She is working today. They are not listening.
Contrast with present simple: I work here (habit) vs I am working now (this moment).
a/an are indefinite (one, not specific); the is definite (specific). Use an before a vowel sound.
Most nouns add -s. Some change spelling or are irregular.
To say that something exists or is present. Use there is for singular, there are for plural.
There is a book on the table. There are two windows in the room.
Common in British English for possession. Have got = have.
I have got a sister. She has got a new phone. Have you got a pen?
can + base verb expresses ability or permission. The same form for all subjects.
I can swim. Can I open the window? She can't drive.

sections.title: Basic prepositions of place and time

rows: in, on, at, under, next to
The keys are on the table.
